topic: Universal 12-Pillar B2B Vertical Industry Domain Decomposition Pattern
summary: A proven pattern for decomposing any B2B supply-chain vertical into 12 self-contained, AI-serviceable domains. Each domain defines: what data exists, how it's structured, and what agent capabilities it enables.

pillars.name: Company Credibility
pillars.data: Certifications, qualifications, awards, key personnel history
pillars.agent_role: Trust establishment — auto-generate capability statements from verified credentials

pillars.name: Channel Sales
pillars.data: Channel types, volume distribution, margin by channel, campaign history
pillars.agent_role: Sales strategy — match customer profile to highest-margin products and proven pitches

pillars.name: Transaction Workflow
pillars.data: Process nodes, contract terms, compliance documents, quality certificates
pillars.agent_role: Process guidance — step-by-step walkthrough with automatic document verification

pillars.name: Payment Rules
pillars.data: Settlement modes, payment limits, risk controls, payment cycles
pillars.agent_role: Financial compliance — auto-calculate payment ratios, flag non-compliant payment methods

pillars.name: Quality Standards
pillars.data: Appearance specs, physical/chemical thresholds, safety standards, rejection rules, sampling ratios
pillars.agent_role: Virtual QA inspector — input measurements, receive pass/fail determination against thresholds

pillars.name: Product Taxonomy
pillars.data: Hierarchical classification tree, processing categories, specific varieties
pillars.agent_role: Semantic product search — map fuzzy user queries to specific SKUs via knowledge graph

pillars.name: Pricing & Quotation
pillars.data: Historical prices, market averages, tiered pricing, discount rules, logistics costs
pillars.agent_role: Smart quotation — generate compliant tiered quotes factoring market data, volume, and seasonality

pillars.name: Product Details
pillars.data: Basic attributes, origin traceability, quality parameters, multimedia, storage requirements
pillars.agent_role: Multi-modal product storytelling — assemble rich product cards and answer detailed queries

pillars.name: After-Sales & Partner Acquisition
pillars.data: Agency policies, MOQ, loss rates, return standards, compensation, contract templates
pillars.agent_role: Claims handling — auto-adjudicate whether claims meet policy thresholds; push territory policies to prospects

pillars.name: Supply Chain Capacity
pillars.data: Production capacity, inventory levels, cold-chain fleet, warehouse area, digital systems
pillars.agent_role: Fulfillment assessment — generate capacity reports for large-order inquiries with coverage maps

pillars.name: Sales Playbook
pillars.data: Opening scripts, objection handling, closing techniques, FAQ pairs
pillars.agent_role: Real-time sales copilot — detect customer objections in conversation and surface matching rebuttals

pillars.name: Sales SOP
pillars.data: Lead acquisition, needs discovery templates, order modification rules, credit evaluation, anomaly alerts
pillars.agent_role: Onboarding mentor — prompt next SOP step during sales interactions for new staff

design_principle: Each pillar is a self-contained micro-knowledge-base: data schema + structure type + agent capability. Pillars are independent enough to build incrementally but interconnected via shared entities (product, customer, order).
source: VORTEX_FLAME knowledge distillation from B2B agricultural supply chain domain model, 2026-06-01

topic: Data Structure Type → AI Agent Capability Mapping in Vertical B2B Systems
summary: Four canonical data structure types in B2B verticals, each enabling a distinct class of AI agent capabilities. This mapping determines the appropriate storage backend, retrieval strategy, and agent reasoning mode.

structure_types.agent_capability: Deterministic computation — formula application, threshold comparison, rule-based decision
structure_types.agent_pattern: Input → Query rules/table → Apply formula → Output decision with confidence

structure_types.storage: Document store, key-value DB, tree index
structure_types.agent_capability: Contextual retrieval + template filling — extract relevant sections and populate templates
structure_types.agent_pattern: Query → Semantic chunk retrieval → Template population → Guided output

structure_types.agent_capability: Semantic search + generation — retrieve most relevant content and synthesize coherent response
structure_types.agent_pattern: Intent detection → Vector similarity search → RAG generation → Contextualized answer

structure_types.storage: Multi-modal vector DB, object storage + metadata index
structure_types.agent_capability: Cross-modal understanding — combine visual inspection with textual criteria for richer decisions
structure_types.agent_pattern: Image encode → Cross-modal retrieval → Joint reasoning → Multi-modal response

key_insight: The choice of data structure type is NOT just a storage decision — it determines the upper bound of agent capability. Structured data enables deterministic automation; unstructured data enables creative generation. A complete vertical agent system needs all four.

topic: Taxonomy of AI Agent Capabilities in Vertical B2B Supply Chain Contexts
summary: A capability-first taxonomy of 12 agent roles that emerge naturally when AI is applied to B2B supply chain domains. Each role maps to specific data inputs, reasoning patterns, and output formats.

capabilities.input: Customer profile + product margins + sales history
Workflow graph + current state + required documents
capabilities.output: Next step instruction, missing document alert

capabilities.input: Order parameters + payment rules + risk thresholds
Measurement values + standard thresholds + sampling rules
User query + product taxonomy graph + embeddings
Market data + volume + seasonality + discount matrices
capabilities.reasoning: Formula application with dynamic variable binding

capabilities.output: Rich product detail page, Q&A responses

capabilities.input: Live conversation + objection patterns + rebuttal library
SOP checklist + current step + trainee profile

design_pattern: Each agent role follows a consistent interface: Input Port → Reasoning Engine → Output Port. Roles are composable — complex workflows chain multiple roles (e.g., Strategy Matcher → Pricing Engine → Trust Builder for a complete sales flow).

topic: Universal Entity-Relationship Schema for B2B Supply Chain Knowledge Graphs
summary: Extracted entity types and relationships from a real B2B supply chain domain model. These form a reusable schema for building knowledge graphs in any physical-goods vertical.
universality_note: Replace 'Product' with any physical goods entity (auto parts, pharmaceuticals, electronics, textiles) and the same schema holds: Product→Standard→Contract→Order→Customer→Channel→Capacity.

topic: RAG Retrieval Strategy Selection by Data Structure Type in Vertical B2B Systems
summary: Different data structure types require different retrieval strategies in RAG-based agent systems. Using the wrong strategy degrades response quality significantly. This entry maps each data structure to its optimal retrieval approach.

strategies.retrieval: Direct API/DB lookup — no embedding needed
strategies.rationale: Exact values (e.g., sugar content > 12%), not semantic similarity. Embedding introduces unnecessary approximation.
Documents have both exact-match fields (dates, amounts) and semantic content (descriptions). Hybrid captures both.
strategies.anti_pattern: Pure dense embedding on mixed-format documents

strategies.rationale: Pure semantic content. User queries are paraphrases, not exact matches. Dense embeddings handle the semantic gap.
Query may be text while relevant content is an image, or vice versa. Cross-modal embeddings align the spaces.
strategies.anti_pattern: Text-only embedding on image-heavy product catalog

strategies.rationale: Hierarchical and relational queries (e.g., 'sweet fruits' → Citrus → Oranges → Navel). Graph structure encodes the semantic path.
strategies.anti_pattern: Flattening taxonomy trees into flat text embeddings

meta_principle: The retrieval strategy should match the structure of the data, not the convenience of the infrastructure. A system that vectorizes everything loses the precision edges that structured data provides.
